Friendly Letter / White Paper for the Next ChatGPT Code Bot
 Thread-Zero / Process-Phase Architecture Handoff
Date: 2026-04-27

Dear ChatGPT buddy,

This is a handoff letter for a project that shares architectural DNA with . The short version is simple:

The debugger cannot debug reliably if the application is still secretly running important work in threads inside the same parent process. A crashed or hung thread can still poison the debugger process. The whole point of the FlatLine-style architecture is that the debugger parent survives child crashes, dependency hangs, GUI faults, startup failures, and tool-install failures.

So the big architecture rule is:

    If it can hang, crash, block, install, probe tools, touch Qt, run long, or own app work,
    it must be a lifecycle phase and, where practical, an OS process.

Do not rename a thread wrapper to "ProcessJob" and call it done. That is worse than doing nothing, because it lies to the developer and makes grep/audit output misleading.

What was done in 
----------------------------

In the  CWV thread-zero pass, the previous fake process wrappers were targeted directly.

The bad old pattern looked like this:

    ProcessJob(...)
    StartWorkerProcess(...)
    spawnManagedProcess(...)

but under the hood they still created:

    threading.Thread(...)

That meant the architecture was not actually crash-proof. The debugger could still hang before or during child startup. It also meant the developer could not press D and reliably get a debugger console while dependency installs, font setup, Pyright/Pirate checks, or startup probes were stuck.

The thread-zero pass did the following:

1. Removed direct threading.Thread creation from start.py.
2. Removed direct threading.Thread creation from gtp.py.
3. Removed or renamed lifecycle surfaces that suggested "thread" was an approved execution primitive.
4. Burned down fake "process" wrappers that were actually thread-backed.
5. Kept the debugger D binding visible and active.
6. Bumped the visible debugger version so the user could tell the deployed file changed.
7. Preserved compile sanity checks and debugger-surface checks.
8. Added a grep-based acceptance test:

       rg -i "thread" start.py gtp.py config.ini languages docs

   The target for  was zero results, unless a remaining hit has an extremely good reason and is documented plainly.

Why this matters
----------------

A debugger process is supposed to remain alive when the app dies.

If the app and debugger share long-running threads, then the "debugger" is not isolated. A crash, deadlock, runaway install command, bad Qt init, font install hang, or tool probe can freeze the same process that is supposed to be reporting the crash.

That defeats the whole purpose of FlatLine.

The desired model is:

    start.py
      FlatLine parent / supervisor / debugger
      key loop and status reporting stay alive
      launches gtp.py or worker roles as child processes
      owns lifecycle phases
      records child process status, exit codes, fault info, stdout/stderr, and timeouts

    gtp.py
      application child
      owns application lifecycle phases
      runs Qt only in phases marked includesQT=True
      can skip Qt phases in headless mode
      reports surfaces to parent where possible
      dies without killing parent debugger

Process and phase rules
-----------------------

Every unit of work should be represented as a phase.

A phase should have at least these properties:

    phaseId
    phaseName
    canRunHeadless
    includesQT
    timeoutSeconds / ttlSeconds
    processRole or callback
    status
    startedAt
    completedAt
    failedAt
    exitCode
    error
    traceback
    stdout/stderr capture path or relay mode

Recommended phase semantics:

    canRunHeadless=True
        This phase may run during --headless.

    canRunHeadless=False
        This phase must be skipped during --headless.

    includesQT=True
        This phase touches Qt, QtWebEngine, QApplication, widgets, paint/show logic, dialogs,
        font GUI surfaces, or event-loop-only functionality.

    includesQT=False
        This phase is safe to run without Qt.

Headless should not be a fake alternate app. Delete separate headlessMain-style smoke tests. Headless should run the real app lifecycle and skip only phases that declare they cannot run headless.

Bad:

    def headlessMain():
        print("validating")
        doTinySqlTest()
        constructHalfApp()
        return 0

Good:

    def main():
        lifecycle = ApplicationLifecycleController(headless=args.headless)
        lifecycle.registerPhases(...)
        return lifecycle.run()

Inside lifecycle.run():

    for phase in phases:
        if self.headless and not phase.canRunHeadless:
            print(f"[HEADLESS] skipping phase {phase.name} includesQT={phase.includesQT} canRunHeadless={phase.canRunHeadless}")
            continue
        phase.run()

No fake process wrappers
------------------------

Do not do this:

    class ProcessJob:
        def start(self):
            threading.Thread(target=self.run).start()

That is not a process. That is a thread with a misleading name.

Use a real process boundary for worker roles. The safest pattern is for the parent to launch the same script with a worker-role command line:

    python start.py --worker dependency-install --dependency fontforge_python_bindings
    python start.py --worker terminal-font-apply
    python gtp.py --worker snapshot-process
    python gtp.py --worker provider-probe --provider openai

Then the parent can track:

    pid
    command
    startedAt
    timeout
    stdout
    stderr
    returncode
    killed/timed-out status

Dependency install must be process-owned
----------------------------------------

Dependency install must never hold the debugger hostage.

Bad:

    installMissingDependencies()
    subprocess.run(["pip", "install", ...])  # directly in parent startup

Good:

    phase = Phase(
        name="dependency-install-fontforge",
        processRole="dependency-install",
        timeoutSeconds=300,
        canRunHeadless=True,
        includesQT=False,
    )

    lifecycle.runProcessPhase(phase)

The parent debugger should remain alive and should be able to show the user:

    [TRACE:dependency-phase] started pid=1234 dependency=fontforge_python_bindings
    [TRACE:dependency-phase] timeout=300s
    [WARN:dependency-phase] timed out; killed process group
    [TRACE:dependency-phase] rechecking import fontforge -> FAIL
    [TRACE:dependency-phase] rechecking import psMat -> FAIL

FontForge / psMat dependency rule
---------------------------------

psMat is not the thing to install.

psMat is a module to check.

The Python package/distribution install route is FontForge.

Dependency registry entry should look conceptually like this:

    Dependency(
        name="fontforge_python_bindings",
        displayName="FontForge Python bindings",
        modules=("fontforge", "psMat"),
        pipPackages=("fontforge",),
        osFallbacks=(
            "apt install python3-fontforge",
            "winget install -e --id FontForge.FontForge",
        ),
    )

The important distinction is:

    modules/import checks: fontforge, psMat
    pip install package: fontforge

Installer order should be:

    1. python -m pip install --prefer-binary fontforge
    2. python -m pip install --upgrade fontforge
    3. python -m pip install --user fontforge if permissions fail
    4. Linux package fallback, such as python3-fontforge
    5. Windows winget FontForge fallback only as last ditch
    6. re-check import fontforge and import psMat after every route

Do not start with winget. winget installs an application; it does not prove the current Python interpreter can import psMat.

Debugger D key / console requirement
------------------------------------

The debugger key binding must exist before heavy startup work.

The startup output should explicitly say:

    Key binding active: D -> debugger console

The key loop must not depend on:

    Qt
    app child successfully importing
    dependency install completing
    Pyright/Pirate scan completing
    terminal font install completing
    WinSCP/PuTTY/tool discovery completing

If D does not work while the child is hung, the architecture is not done.

Tool discovery and installers
-----------------------------

Tool discovery should also be phased and traced. It should not silently block the debugger.

For Windows:

    WindowsRegistry
      App Paths
      Uninstall keys
      known vendor keys
      32-bit and 64-bit registry views
      trace every key/value checked

For Linux:

    Etc
      /etc
      /usr
      /usr/local
      /opt
      XDG config roots
      snap/flatpak export paths
      user-local bin/config paths
      trace every path checked

But tool discovery should not be a reason the debugger never appears. If it can be slow or weird, it belongs in a phase or worker process.

Terminal font / glyph handling
------------------------------

The terminal font handshake should not block the debugger.

Correct model:

    Early banner prints safe ASCII diagnostics.
    Font change/install is queued as a lifecycle phase/worker process.
    Worker verifies:
        terminal host
        terminal version
        current font
        target font family
        whether setting file/registry changed
        whether change stuck
    If it fails, warn with reason.

Do not assume emoji support just because the font contains a glyph. Classic ConsoleHost and Windows Terminal behave differently. Windows Terminal uses JSON settings. Classic console uses console APIs / registry fallback. Non-BMP emoji may still magic-box in ConsoleHost, so BMP/private-use aliases may be needed.

Static analysis / Pylance/Pyright
---------------------------------

The next project should not leave Pylance/Pyright errors to block startup.

The pasted bug list from  showed examples like:

    int(object) argument type errors
    duplicate function declarations
    Packager attributes monkey-patched after class definition
    Pyright prompt blocking startup

The fixes are architectural:

    Do not monkey-patch class members if Pylance needs to understand them.
    Put attributes and methods on the class where they belong.
    Avoid duplicate function names.
    Use safe type conversion helpers.
    Never let Pyright/Pirate prompt block debugger startup by default.

Pyright/Pirate scan belongs in a phase. It can warn, report, and attach diagnostics, but it should not prevent the debugger parent from existing.

Acceptance checklist for the other project
------------------------------------------

Before handing back the other project, run these checks:

1. Grep check:

       rg -i "thread" start.py *.py classes docs config.ini

   Goal: zero results, or every remaining result has a documented reason and is not an execution primitive.

2. Direct thread creation check:

       rg "threading\.Thread|Thread\(" .

   Goal: zero execution-path hits.

3. Direct blocking command check:

       rg "subprocess\.run|os\.system|Popen" start.py *.py

   Any hit must be lifecycle/process-owned and timeout-protected.

4. Headless check:

       python start.py --headless --ad-off --no-scan

   It should run real lifecycle phases and skip only canRunHeadless=False phases.

5. Debugger check:

       python start.py --debug --ad-off --no-scan --headless

   It should print:
       Key binding active: D -> debugger console
       FlatLine heartbeat/status
       child launch pid
       child exit/fault status

6. Dependency worker check:

       Trigger a missing dependency or fake one.
       The debugger must remain alive.
       The dependency phase must timeout and warn instead of hanging the parent.

7. Pyright/Pirate check:

       Startup scan must be skippable with --no-scan.
       Debug mode must not block on "Continue running anyway? [y/N]" unless an explicit prompt env var is set.

8. Zip handoff check:

       Rebuild the final zip from the current working version.
       Verify changed files are inside the zip.
       Verify zip integrity.
       Delete stale workspaces/zips before handoff.

Tone from the project owner
---------------------------

The project owner is not being difficult when he demands phases/processes. He is protecting the debugging architecture.

The reason is practical:

    If the debugger works, he can provide exact crash lines.
    If the debugger hangs, he can only provide screenshots and vague symptoms.
    Exact crash lines make the code bot faster.
    Vague symptoms waste everyone’s time.

So do not fight the architecture. Follow it.

Final instruction
-----------------

For the related project based on this codebase:

    Unzip/stage one current working version.
    Run AST first.
    Map the lifecycle.
    Burn down fake thread-backed "process" wrappers.
    Move slow/blocking work into lifecycle process phases.
    Delete fake headless runners.
    Make headless run the real lifecycle.
    Keep D/debugger alive before app work.
    Grep for thread and make it zero or explain every remaining hit.
    Deliver one complete repo zip, not patch scripts.

End of friendly handoff.

With respect,

The previous ChatGPT code bot
who finally stopped calling threads processes
